Should genetic screening of embryos be limited to preventing serious medical conditions, or should parents have broader freedom to select traits?

students.name: Mira
students.text: I believe genetic screening should be strictly limited to serious medical conditions. Allowing parents to select non-medical traits would create a society where children are designed to meet parental preferences rather than accepted for who they are. This could deepen social inequality, as wealthier families would have access to genetic advantages that others cannot afford. We risk reducing human reproduction to a consumer transaction, which fundamentally changes how we value human life.

students.name: Tyler
students.text: I think parents should have broader freedom in genetic screening because restricting reproductive choices sets a dangerous precedent for government control over family decisions. Parents already make countless choices that shape their children's futures, from nutrition to education. If the technology exists to give a child the best possible start in life, it seems arbitrary to allow screening for some conditions but not others. Clear ethical guidelines can prevent extreme abuses without eliminating parental choice entirely.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As cities expand and traffic grows more congested, transportation planners debate the best long-term solution for urban mobility. Some advocate for investing heavily in autonomous vehicle technology, arguing that self-driving cars will reduce accidents and optimize traffic flow. Others believe that cities should instead invest in expanding rail and bus rapid transit systems, which have a proven track record. Which investment do you think will have the greater impact on urban transportation over the next twenty years?

students.name: Chloe
students.text: I believe autonomous vehicles will have the greater impact because they can transform existing road infrastructure without massive construction projects. Self-driving cars can communicate with each other to reduce congestion, eliminate human error that causes most accidents, and provide door-to-door service that public transit cannot match. As the technology matures and costs decrease, autonomous vehicles will become accessible to most urban residents and fundamentally change how cities manage transportation.

students.name: Kofi
students.text: Public transit investment will have a far greater impact because autonomous cars still take up road space and contribute to congestion. A single bus can replace fifty individual cars on the road, and rail systems move thousands of people per hour more efficiently than any car-based technology. Cities with excellent public transit like Tokyo and Singapore already demonstrate that trains and buses are the most effective solution for dense urban areas. Autonomous vehicles are a technological distraction from proven infrastructure solutions.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The fashion industry is the second-largest polluter globally, and the rise of fast fashion has accelerated environmental damage through overproduction and textile waste. Some designers advocate for a return to slow fashion, producing fewer but higher-quality garments at higher prices. Others argue that affordable clothing is a necessity and that the industry should focus on sustainable materials and circular production methods instead. What approach do you think is most realistic for making the fashion industry more sustainable?

students.name: Zara
students.text: Slow fashion is the most realistic path to sustainability because it addresses the root problem of overconsumption. When people invest in well-made clothing that lasts years instead of buying cheap items they discard after a few wears, total textile waste decreases dramatically. Brands like Patagonia have shown that consumers will pay more for durable, ethically produced clothing. We need to shift the cultural mindset from treating clothes as disposable to valuing craftsmanship and longevity.

students.name: Andre
students.text: Slow fashion is unrealistic because many people simply cannot afford expensive clothing. Telling low-income families to buy fewer, pricier garments ignores economic reality. A more practical approach is requiring fast fashion companies to use recycled materials, implement take-back programs, and invest in textile recycling technology. This allows affordable clothing to remain available while reducing the environmental footprint of production. Sustainability should not be a luxury that only wealthy consumers can participate in.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The use of animals in scientific research remains one of the most debated topics in both science and ethics. While animal testing has contributed to major medical breakthroughs, including vaccines and surgical techniques, new alternatives like organ-on-a-chip technology and computer modeling are becoming increasingly sophisticated. Some scientists argue that animal testing is still irreplaceable for understanding complex biological systems, while others believe we have reached a point where alternatives can largely replace animal models. Should scientific institutions set firm timelines for phasing out animal testing?

students.name: Suki
students.text: Yes, setting firm timelines would accelerate the development and adoption of alternative methods. Without deadlines, institutions have little incentive to invest in new technologies when established animal testing protocols already exist. The European Union's push to reduce animal testing has spurred remarkable innovation in computational biology and tissue engineering. A phased timeline gives researchers time to adapt while sending a clear signal that the scientific community takes animal welfare seriously.

students.name: Nathan
students.text: Firm timelines could be dangerous for scientific progress and public health. Some areas of research, particularly drug safety testing and immunology, still require whole-organism studies that alternatives cannot fully replicate. Rushing to eliminate animal models before replacements are validated could lead to unsafe medications reaching patients. I think institutions should aggressively fund alternative methods and reduce animal use wherever possible, but arbitrary deadlines risk compromising the quality of research that saves human lives.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Archaeological excavation methods have evolved significantly over the past century. Traditional approaches involve physically digging through layers of soil, carefully documenting each artifact's position. However, modern technologies like ground-penetrating radar and LIDAR allow researchers to map buried structures without disturbing the ground at all. Some archaeologists argue that non-invasive methods should become the default approach, while others believe that physical excavation remains essential for a complete understanding of a site. What do you think is the better primary approach for modern archaeology?

students.name: Soren
students.text: I believe non-invasive technologies should be the primary method. Every time we dig, we permanently alter or destroy parts of the archaeological record. Future generations may have even better tools to analyze sites we haven't touched. Using remote sensing first lets us make smarter decisions about where and whether to excavate at all.

students.name: Adaeze
students.text: While technology is impressive, I think physical excavation must remain central to archaeology. Remote sensing can show us shapes and structures underground, but it cannot reveal the textures, organic materials, or subtle details that only hands-on work uncovers. Many of our greatest discoveries came from direct contact with artifacts in their original context.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The pharmaceutical industry faces ongoing debate about drug pricing. Brand-name medications are often extremely expensive because companies invest billions in research, clinical trials, and regulatory approval. Generic drugs offer the same active ingredients at a fraction of the cost but can only be produced after patents expire. Some argue that patent periods should be shortened to make medications affordable sooner, while others say strong patent protection is necessary to incentivize innovation. Should pharmaceutical patent periods be reduced to improve public access to affordable medicine?

students.name: Beatriz
students.text: I strongly support shorter patent periods. People around the world suffer and even die because they cannot afford essential medications. If generic versions could enter the market sooner, prices would drop dramatically. The current system prioritizes corporate profits over human lives, and that is a moral failure we need to address urgently.

students.name: Henrik
students.text: Shortening patents sounds compassionate, but it could backfire. Developing a new drug costs an average of two billion dollars, and companies need time to recoup that investment. Without strong patent protection, pharmaceutical firms would have less incentive to fund risky research into new treatments. We could end up with cheaper drugs today but fewer breakthroughs tomorrow.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Forest management strategies differ widely depending on priorities. Some foresters advocate for selective logging, where only certain trees are harvested while the rest of the ecosystem remains intact. Others support clear-cutting designated areas followed by replanting, arguing it is more efficient and can actually benefit certain species that thrive in open environments. Both methods have economic and ecological implications. In your view, which timber harvesting approach is more sustainable for long-term forest health?

students.name: Tariq
students.text: Selective logging is clearly the more sustainable option for long-term forest management. It preserves biodiversity by maintaining the forest canopy and protecting habitat for countless species. Clear-cutting devastates ecosystems, causes soil erosion, and disrupts water cycles. Even with replanting, it takes decades for a clear-cut area to resemble a mature forest again.

students.name: Astrid
students.text: I think clear-cutting gets an unfairly bad reputation. When managed responsibly with proper replanting programs, it can actually create diverse habitats. Some tree species and wildlife depend on open, sunlit areas that only exist after clearing. Selective logging can also damage forests through repeated equipment access and trail creation over many years.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Video game designers constantly balance artistic vision with player expectations. Some developers create games with extremely high difficulty levels, arguing that overcoming tough challenges provides a deeper sense of accomplishment. Others believe games should include adjustable difficulty settings so that a wider audience can enjoy the experience regardless of skill level. This tension has sparked heated discussion in the gaming community. Should game designers prioritize accessibility by including difficulty options, or is maintaining a single intended difficulty level a valid artistic choice?

students.name: Bodhi
students.text: Games are an art form, and artists should have the freedom to design the experience they envision. A single difficulty level ensures every player shares the same journey and emotional arc. Adding easy modes can undermine the game's core message. Films do not offer simplified plots for casual viewers, and games should not compromise their vision either.

students.name: Ines
students.text: I disagree. Including difficulty options does not diminish artistic vision; it expands who can appreciate it. Players with disabilities, limited time, or less gaming experience deserve to enjoy these stories and worlds too. Accessibility features in other media, like subtitles or audio descriptions, are celebrated. Games should embrace the same inclusive philosophy.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Housing affordability is a pressing concern in many cities worldwide. One proposed solution is rent control, which limits how much landlords can increase rent each year. Supporters say it protects tenants from being priced out of their neighborhoods. Critics argue that rent control discourages new construction and reduces the overall housing supply, ultimately making the problem worse. Based on what we have discussed about housing markets, do you think rent control is an effective policy for addressing housing affordability?

students.name: Rohan
students.text: Rent control provides immediate relief to vulnerable tenants who would otherwise face displacement. In rapidly gentrifying neighborhoods, it is often the only thing keeping communities intact. While it may not solve every housing problem, protecting existing residents from sudden, dramatic rent increases is a legitimate and necessary policy goal that markets alone cannot achieve.

students.name: Maeve
students.text: Rent control treats the symptom rather than the disease. Economic research consistently shows that it reduces housing supply over time because developers and landlords have less incentive to build or maintain properties. Instead of capping rents, cities should focus on zoning reform and incentives for new construction to increase the total housing stock available.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The rise of citizen journalism through social media has transformed how news is reported and consumed. Ordinary people with smartphones can now document events in real time, sometimes reaching audiences before professional journalists arrive on the scene. However, citizen journalists typically lack formal training in verification, ethics, and balanced reporting. Some media scholars welcome this democratization of news, while others worry about the spread of misinformation. Do you believe citizen journalism strengthens or weakens the quality of public information?

students.name: Lucian
students.text: Citizen journalism is a powerful force for accountability. Professional media outlets have their own biases and blind spots, and they cannot be everywhere at once. When ordinary people share footage of police misconduct or natural disasters, they fill critical gaps in coverage. The public benefits from having more sources of information, not fewer.

students.name: Amara
students.text: The problem with citizen journalism is that anyone can publish anything without verification. Professional journalists follow ethical codes, check sources, and provide context. Citizen reports often lack these safeguards, leading to rumors and misinformation spreading rapidly. The public may feel more informed, but the quality of that information is frequently unreliable.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Classical dance forms like ballet have strict technical standards that dancers must master through years of rigorous training. Contemporary dance, by contrast, often values individual expression and innovation over established technique. Some dance educators believe that all serious dancers should first build a strong foundation in classical technique before exploring contemporary styles. Others argue that requiring classical training can limit creativity and exclude dancers whose bodies or backgrounds do not fit traditional molds. Should classical training be considered essential for all professional dancers?

students.name: Nia
students.text: Classical training provides discipline, strength, and body awareness that benefit any style of dance. Learning ballet fundamentals gives dancers a shared vocabulary and physical control that translates across genres. Even the most innovative contemporary choreographers often have classical backgrounds. Starting with strong technique does not limit creativity; it gives dancers more tools to express themselves.

students.name: Idris
students.text: Requiring classical training as a foundation is an outdated and exclusionary idea. Many brilliant dancers come from hip-hop, folk, or cultural traditions that have their own rigorous techniques. Insisting on ballet as the starting point centers one particular Western tradition and marginalizes others. Dance education should honor multiple pathways to excellence.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The nursing profession is experiencing a significant staffing shortage in many countries. One debated solution is increasing the use of travel nurses, who accept temporary assignments at hospitals facing shortages, often at much higher pay than permanent staff. Hospitals benefit from immediate staffing relief, but permanent nurses sometimes feel undervalued when temporary colleagues earn substantially more for similar work. This can affect morale and retention. Is the widespread use of travel nurses a good solution to the nursing shortage?

students.name: Dalia
students.text: Travel nursing addresses urgent needs that cannot wait for long-term solutions. When hospitals are dangerously understaffed, patient safety is at risk. Travel nurses step in quickly and bring diverse experience from working at multiple facilities. The higher pay reflects the personal sacrifices of constant relocation and the market demand for their flexibility.

students.name: Callum
students.text: Relying on travel nurses is a short-sighted approach that actually worsens the shortage. When permanent staff see travel nurses earning double or triple their salary, many quit to become travelers themselves. This creates a destructive cycle. Hospitals should invest that money in raising permanent staff wages and improving working conditions to retain their core nursing teams.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Global supply chains have proven vulnerable to disruption, as seen during recent pandemics and geopolitical conflicts. Many companies are now considering reshoring, which means bringing manufacturing back to their home countries rather than relying on distant overseas factories. Reshoring can reduce shipping delays and political risks but typically increases production costs. Some experts advocate for a middle ground called nearshoring, moving production to nearby countries instead. Should companies prioritize reshoring their supply chains, even at higher cost?

students.name: Petra
students.text: Reshoring is worth the higher cost because supply chain resilience is priceless. The disruptions we have witnessed showed how fragile global networks can be. Having production closer to home means faster response times, better quality control, and reduced dependence on politically unstable regions. Companies should view reshoring as a long-term strategic investment rather than a short-term expense.

students.name: Mateo
students.text: Completely reshoring is impractical for most companies. The cost difference is simply too large, and consumers are not willing to pay significantly higher prices. A smarter approach is diversifying suppliers across multiple countries and regions. This reduces risk without abandoning the economic advantages of global trade. Total reshoring would raise prices and could even slow economic growth.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Plastic has become one of the most versatile and widely used materials in modern life, but its environmental impact is severe. Researchers are developing biodegradable alternatives made from plant-based materials like corn starch or seaweed. However, these bioplastics currently cost more to produce, sometimes perform worse than traditional plastics, and may require specific industrial conditions to decompose properly. Should governments mandate a transition from conventional plastics to biodegradable alternatives, even given current limitations?

students.name: Leila
students.text: We cannot wait for perfect alternatives before acting. Plastic pollution is devastating our oceans and ecosystems right now. Government mandates would accelerate investment in bioplastic research, driving costs down and performance up over time. The initial limitations are growing pains that will be resolved much faster with regulatory pressure pushing the industry forward.

students.name: Nikolai
students.text: Mandating a switch before bioplastics are truly ready could cause more harm than good. If biodegradable plastics do not decompose properly outside industrial facilities, we just create a different pollution problem. A better approach is funding research and offering incentives for development while improving recycling infrastructure for existing plastics in the meantime.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Deep-sea mining has emerged as a potential way to extract valuable minerals like cobalt, nickel, and manganese from the ocean floor. These minerals are essential for batteries and renewable energy technologies. However, marine scientists warn that mining could cause irreversible damage to fragile deep-sea ecosystems that we barely understand. The deep ocean hosts unique species and plays important roles in carbon cycling. Should deep-sea mining be permitted to support the transition to renewable energy, or should the ocean floor remain off-limits?

students.name: Freya
students.text: The deep ocean is Earth's last truly unexplored frontier, and we should not destroy it before we even understand it. Deep-sea ecosystems recover incredibly slowly, if at all. We should invest in recycling existing minerals, developing alternative battery technologies, and mining responsibly on land rather than opening up an entirely new environment to industrial extraction.

students.name: Darius
students.text: The green energy transition requires enormous quantities of specific minerals, and land-based mining alone cannot meet that demand. Deep-sea mining, if carefully regulated, could provide these critical resources with a smaller surface footprint than terrestrial mines. Blocking it entirely could actually slow the shift away from fossil fuels, which poses a far greater environmental threat.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: When addressing homelessness, communities generally pursue one of two broad strategies. The housing-first model provides homeless individuals with permanent housing immediately, without requiring them to meet conditions like sobriety or employment first. The traditional approach requires individuals to complete treatment programs and demonstrate readiness before receiving housing. Housing-first has shown promising results in some cities, but critics question whether it addresses root causes of homelessness. Which approach do you think is more effective for reducing homelessness long-term?

students.name: Tessa
students.text: Housing-first is clearly more effective because it recognizes that stability is a prerequisite for recovery, not a reward for it. Research shows that people are far more likely to address addiction, mental health, and employment challenges when they have a safe, permanent place to live. Requiring readiness first keeps people trapped in a cycle of shelters and streets.

students.name: Joaquin
students.text: Housing-first sounds ideal, but without addressing the underlying issues that caused homelessness, we risk simply relocating problems rather than solving them. Some individuals need structured treatment programs to develop the skills necessary for maintaining housing independently. Providing housing without adequate support services can lead to property damage, eviction, and a return to homelessness.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Fossil specimens are invaluable scientific resources, yet many end up in private collections after being discovered on private land. In some countries, fossils found on private property legally belong to the landowner, who may sell them to the highest bidder. Scientists argue that important specimens should be accessible for research in public institutions. Landowners counter that they should benefit financially from discoveries on their own property. Should governments require that scientifically significant fossils be placed in public museums rather than sold to private collectors?

students.name: Thandi
students.text: Scientifically significant fossils belong to all of humanity, not just whoever owns the land they are found on. Once a rare specimen disappears into a private collection, it is lost to science forever. Governments should require that major fossil finds be deposited in public institutions where researchers and the public can study and appreciate them.

students.name: Felix
students.text: Forcing landowners to surrender fossils without fair compensation would actually discourage people from reporting discoveries. Many important finds are made by amateur collectors and farmers who invest time and resources in extraction. If the government can simply take their finds, people will hide or ignore specimens. A better approach is offering fair purchase prices and tax incentives.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The rapid growth of short-term rental platforms like Airbnb has disrupted the traditional hotel industry and transformed neighborhoods in tourist destinations. Supporters say these platforms provide travelers with affordable, authentic accommodation options while allowing homeowners to earn extra income. Critics argue that short-term rentals reduce available housing for local residents, drive up rents, and disturb residential neighborhoods. Should cities impose strict regulations on short-term rental platforms to protect housing availability and neighborhood character?

students.name: Yara
students.text: Cities absolutely need to regulate short-term rentals strictly. In popular tourist destinations, entire apartment buildings have been converted into informal hotels, displacing long-term residents and hollowing out communities. Local families should not have to compete with tourists for housing. Reasonable regulations like limiting the number of rental days per year can balance tourism income with residents' needs.

students.name: Finn
students.text: Heavy regulation stifles economic opportunity and consumer choice. Many hosts are ordinary people renting out a spare room to supplement their income, not corporate landlords. Strict rules often punish small-scale hosts while doing little to address larger housing affordability issues. Cities should focus on building more housing rather than restricting how people use their own property.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As robots and artificial intelligence become more capable, they are increasingly being used in caregiving roles, particularly for elderly individuals and people with disabilities. Robotic caregivers can assist with physical tasks, medication reminders, and even basic companionship. Proponents argue that robots can help address the global shortage of human caregivers. However, some ethicists worry that replacing human connection with machine interaction could harm the emotional well-being of vulnerable people. Is it ethical to use robots as primary caregivers for elderly or disabled individuals?

students.name: Karim
students.text: Robotic caregivers should supplement human care, not replace it. The caregiver shortage is real, and robots can handle physical tasks like lifting patients or dispensing medication reliably. This frees human caregivers to focus on emotional support and complex decision-making. Used thoughtfully, robots can actually improve the quality of care that vulnerable people receive.

students.name: Sienna
students.text: Relying on robots for caregiving is a deeply troubling path for society to take. Elderly and disabled individuals often suffer most from loneliness and isolation. A machine cannot provide genuine empathy, comfort, or meaningful conversation. Normalizing robotic care risks sending the message that these vulnerable populations do not deserve real human attention and connection.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: DNA databases maintained by law enforcement have become powerful tools for solving crimes, including cold cases that went unsolved for decades. Some countries are expanding these databases to include DNA samples from anyone arrested, not just those convicted of crimes. Advocates say broader databases increase the chances of identifying perpetrators. Civil liberties groups warn that collecting DNA from people who have not been convicted violates privacy rights and disproportionately affects certain communities. Should law enforcement be allowed to collect and retain DNA from arrested individuals who are not convicted?

students.name: Esme
students.text: Collecting DNA from arrested individuals goes too far. Being arrested does not mean someone is guilty, and retaining their genetic information permanently treats them as suspects for life. This practice disproportionately impacts communities that are already over-policed. DNA is deeply personal information, and the government should only retain it after a conviction, not merely an arrest.

students.name: Rashid
students.text: Expanded DNA databases save lives by solving crimes faster and even preventing future offenses. The process is no more invasive than fingerprinting, which has been standard practice for decades. If someone is truly innocent, having their DNA on file should not concern them. The potential to bring justice to victims and their families outweighs the minimal privacy intrusion.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Urban parks and green spaces are increasingly recognized as essential infrastructure, not just amenities. However, cities face a paradox: investing in new parks and improving existing ones often increases surrounding property values, which can displace the low-income residents who would benefit most from green space access. This phenomenon is sometimes called green gentrification. How should cities approach the development of urban green spaces while preventing displacement of vulnerable communities?

students.name: Vivian
students.text: Cities should pair green space investments with strong anti-displacement policies like affordable housing requirements and rent stabilization near new parks. The solution is not to avoid building parks in underserved neighborhoods but to protect existing residents while improving their environment. With proper planning, green infrastructure and social equity can coexist rather than conflict.

students.name: Oren
students.text: I think a more effective approach is distributing many small green interventions throughout neighborhoods rather than building large showcase parks. Smaller community gardens, green corridors, and pocket parks improve quality of life without attracting the kind of real estate speculation that drives gentrification. This decentralized strategy brings nature to people without transforming entire neighborhoods.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Artificial intelligence tools can now generate logos, layouts, and illustrations in seconds based on simple text prompts. Some designers embrace these tools as productivity boosters that handle routine tasks, freeing them to focus on higher-level creative strategy. Others fear that AI-generated design devalues the profession and threatens designers' livelihoods. Clients may increasingly question why they should pay for human designers when AI can produce acceptable results at a fraction of the cost. Will AI tools ultimately help or harm the graphic design profession?

students.name: Lena
students.text: AI will elevate the design profession, not destroy it. Just as desktop publishing did not eliminate designers in the 1990s, AI will shift the role toward more strategic and conceptual work. Designers who learn to use AI as a tool will become more productive and valuable. The profession will evolve, and those who adapt will thrive in this new landscape.

students.name: Kwesi
students.text: I am genuinely worried about the impact on designers, especially those starting their careers. Entry-level design work like creating simple logos or social media graphics is exactly what AI does best. If junior positions disappear, how will the next generation of designers gain experience? The profession could become increasingly elitist, accessible only to those who can afford years of unpaid creative development.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Nuclear power is experiencing renewed interest as countries seek carbon-free energy sources to combat climate change. Modern reactor designs are significantly safer than older ones, and nuclear plants produce enormous amounts of reliable, emissions-free electricity. However, concerns persist about radioactive waste storage, the high cost of construction, and the risk of catastrophic accidents, however unlikely. Some environmentalists now support nuclear energy, while others insist that solar and wind power alone can meet our needs. Should nuclear energy play a major role in the transition away from fossil fuels?

students.name: Sasha
students.text: Nuclear energy is essential for decarbonization. Solar and wind are intermittent and require massive energy storage solutions that do not yet exist at scale. Nuclear provides constant, reliable baseload power regardless of weather conditions. The waste issue is manageable with modern storage technology, and newer reactor designs have made meltdown scenarios virtually impossible. We cannot afford to exclude nuclear from our energy mix.

students.name: Juno
students.text: Nuclear power is too expensive, too slow to build, and too risky to be the answer. New nuclear plants take a decade or more to construct and cost billions more than projected. Meanwhile, solar and wind costs have plummeted and continue falling. Investing in renewable energy plus battery storage is faster, cheaper, and does not create radioactive waste that remains dangerous for thousands of years.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Major museums in Western countries hold millions of artifacts taken from other nations during colonial periods. In recent years, some institutions have begun returning objects to their countries of origin, acknowledging that these items were often acquired through force or exploitation. Others argue that museums serve as universal repositories of human heritage and that dispersing collections would reduce public access to important cultural objects. Should Western museums be required to return artifacts acquired during colonial eras to their countries of origin?

students.name: Aaliya
students.text: These artifacts were taken without consent during periods of exploitation and violence. Keeping them in Western museums perpetuates colonial power dynamics. Countries of origin have the right to their own cultural heritage, and many now have excellent museums capable of preserving and displaying these objects. Returning artifacts is not just a cultural act but a moral obligation and a step toward justice.

students.name: Conrad
students.text: While I understand the moral argument, returning every colonial-era artifact could be impractical and even harmful. Some originating countries lack the infrastructure to preserve fragile objects, and disputed ownership between modern nations complicates matters. Museums could instead pursue shared custody arrangements, rotating exhibitions, and digital access programs that honor cultural heritage while maintaining preservation standards.
